ABOUT

Background

About Me

I’m a Materials Science graduate student at Purdue University (GPA 3.7, graduating May 2026), specializing in semiconductor process engineering and battery cell development. My work combines materials characterization, data-driven process optimization, and mechanical design, bridging lab work and manufacturing at scale.

At AMD’s Austin facility, I optimized HBM wafer yield by analyzing 10,000+ data points and building Python automation tools that cut root cause analysis time by over 60%. I traced yield-limiting defects to Czochralski oxygen contamination at wafer edges and developed a die placement algorithm that recovered 15%+ yield loss, saving millions in scrap costs.

My background spans semiconductor packaging (thin-film deposition, SPC, DOE), polymer characterization (FT-IR, DSC, TGA, TMA, DMA), and Li-ion battery design. I’ve designed thermal test fixtures using SolidWorks Flow Simulation and FEA, optimized biopharmaceutical manufacturing through statistical modeling, and built mechanical systems from CAD to fabrication.

I hold a BS in Mechanical Engineering from the University of Twente, Netherlands (GPA 7.5/10.0) with an Honors certification (top 10% of cohort). I’m actively seeking full-time roles in semiconductor process engineering, battery manufacturing, or materials R&D.

HBMWafer YieldPythonNumPyPandasMatplotlibSQLJMPSPCDOECVD

Background

AMD faced critical yield issues with 12-layer High Bandwidth Memory (HBM) stacks sourced from multiple OSAT suppliers. With 12 vertical die layers, the theoretical yield target is 0.98¹² = 78%; even a 1% per-layer drop compounds dramatically across the stack.

Python Automation Pipeline

# Wafer yield heatmap pipeline (simplified) import pandas as pd, numpy as np, matplotlib.pyplot as plt, seaborn as sns # 1. Extract from SQL data warehouse df = pd.read_sql("SELECT die_x, die_y, yield, wafer_id FROM hbm_test WHERE lot=?", conn) # 2. Spatial correlation: radial distance from center df['radius'] = np.sqrt(df.die_x**2 + df.die_y**2) df['edge_flag'] = df.radius > (df.radius.max() * 0.85) # 3. Heatmap: color-coded by yield, expose edge pattern pivot = df.pivot_table('yield', 'die_y', 'die_x') sns.heatmap(pivot, cmap='RdYlGn', vmin=0.85, vmax=1.0)

Built Python pipeline tracing data through entire manufacturing process (package test → die assembly → wafer test → supplier data), generating spatial heatmaps from 10,000+ data points per wafer. Used 5-Why analysis to systematically drill down to root cause.

Root Cause: Czochralski O₂ Contamination

In the Czochralski process, oxygen from the quartz crucible dissolves into the silicon melt and concentrates at crystal edges (the last regions to solidify). During thermal processing, these oxygen-rich zones form SiO₂ precipitates that nucleate defects, producing the 93% edge vs 99–100% center yield gradient. SIMS data from the wafer supplier confirmed the contamination hypothesis; SPC p-charts were implemented for early warning monitoring, with projected ~15% yield recovery if findings are implemented.

Die Placement Algorithm & Statistical Methods

  • Computed radial distance per die; flagged high-risk candidates beyond 85% of wafer radius
  • Exclusion list fed to assembly before die attach; SPC p-charts tracked yield improvement by lot
  • DOE + regression to isolate process variables and correlate O₂ proxy metrics with yield loss
  • Cpk capability analysis: Cpk 1.6 → 1.8 over production run

Impact & Outcomes

  • Python tool adopted as standard workflow by the packaging engineering team
  • Established precedent for data-driven yield investigation across multiple HBM product lines
  • SPC improvements validated sustained yield gains across subsequent production lots

HBM wafer yield heatmap — spatial defect distribution identifying Czochralski oxygen contamination at wafer edges

R&D Materials Engineer Internship

Plastic Recycling Inc (PRI) · Indianapolis, Indiana · Materials R&D

  • —Characterized thermoplastic polymer systems using FTIR, DSC, TGA, DMA, and TMA; built thermal-behavior database of 200+ experimental results, improving material qualification by 17%.
  • —Operated and maintained 5 analytical instruments through daily calibrations; trained manufacturing operators on instrument protocols.

FT-IRDSCTGATMADMADOEJMPTwin-Screw ExtrusionRheometryThermal Analysis

Full Description

Characterized thermoplastic polymer systems using FTIR, DSC, TGA, DMA, and TMA to evaluate thermal and mechanical performance for manufacturing applications, supporting material selection and formula evaluation for recycled polymer development and processing. Built a thermal-behavior database of 200+ experimental results that improved comparative material qualification and process decision-making by 17%.

Worked primarily in an equipment focused R&D role, operating and maintaining 5 analytical instruments through daily calibrations and hands-on failure troubleshooting, while training manufacturing operators on instrument protocols and procedures. Designed and executed a 3-factor, 18-run DOE on twin screw extrusion parameters using JMP (ANOVA, main effects, interactions), identifying temperature profile as the dominant variable; ±5°C deviations caused significant viscosity swings confirmed through rheometry.

Characterization Techniques Applied

  • FT-IR Spectroscopy: Identified functional groups, confirmed polymer identity, detected contamination signals in recycled feedstocks
  • DSC (Differential Scanning Calorimetry): Measured T𝘨, Tₘ, crystallinity, critical for process temperature window definition
  • TGA (Thermogravimetric Analysis): Characterized thermal degradation onset, moisture content, ash residues for recyclability assessment
  • TMA (Thermomechanical Analysis): Measured CTE, softening point, dimensional stability under load
  • DMA (Dynamic Mechanical Analysis): Characterized viscoelastic behavior (storage modulus E′, loss modulus E′′, tan δ) across temperature range

Sample Preparation with Waterjet CNC

Operated the Maxiem waterjet CNC system to machine polymer and composite parts into precise geometries as a pre-processing step before FTIR and thermal analysis. As a cold-cutting process, waterjet machining avoids heat-affected zones and preserves chemical structure, ensuring the material reaching the instrument is unaltered — critical for reliable ATR and transmission FTIR results. Produced flat, dimensionally consistent sections from molded parts and sheet stock, eliminating surface damage that conventional sawing or laser cutting would otherwise introduce.

Maxiem waterjet CNC machining interface — polymer specimen cutting for FTIR sample prep

Research Overview

Collaborated with NIIMBL on the N-mAb integrated continuous bioprocess case study, applying DOE, Bayesian hierarchical modeling, Monte Carlo simulation, and RTD modeling to optimize continuous biomanufacturing operations. Established a Bayesian-informed perfusion control strategy with 95% confidence bounds on glycan CQAs, achieved a 3-fold improvement in HMWS clearance validated across chromatography cycles, and produced 7 FDA-aligned process design recommendations spanning clinical through commercial manufacturing. The statistical frameworks developed are directly transferable to semiconductor process engineering, where the same methods govern yield optimization and process window definition.

Continuous Process Architecture

The N-mAb case study (NIIMBL) examines two integrated continuous bioprocess architectures distinguished by how intermediate batching is handled between upstream and downstream operations. Both route material from a steady-state or dynamic perfusion bioreactor through multi-column Protein A capture, inline low-pH viral inactivation, polishing chromatography (flow-through and bind-elute), and UF/DF formulation. A key design element is the use of surge tanks and cycle surge tanks to decouple upstream flow variability from downstream unit operations, functionally analogous to buffer reservoirs used in semiconductor wet process lines to absorb upstream variation before critical deposition or etch steps.

N-mAb integrated continuous bioprocess — Option 1 & Option 2 process schematics (NIIMBL poster, 2022)

Statistical Methods Applied

  • DOE / RSM: Mapped pH, conductivity & residence time for Protein A capture; significant effects at p < 0.05
  • Bayesian Hierarchical Modeling: Characterized CQA distributions (high mannose, G2F) with 95% confidence perfusion control bounds
  • Monte Carlo Simulation: Propagated parameter uncertainty to forecast CQA distributions under varying perfusion conditions
  • RTD Modeling + Python Dashboard: Modeled surge tank traceability; built interactive Plotly app for stakeholder exploration

Rubber CharacterizationML ScorchRheometryTensile TestingRebound ResiliencePolymer MWDVulcanizationStatistical Correlation

Investigated how polymer supplier variation propagates through the tire manufacturing process chain (raw polymer → Banbury compounding → extrusion → calendering → vulcanization) and affects final compound properties. Used ML Scorch, ODR rheometry, and tensile testing (ISO 37) to characterize each stage. Key finding: molecular weight distribution (MWD) of the incoming polymer — specifically the high-MW tail — was the strongest predictor of compound processability and tensile performance, directly informing Apollo’s supplier qualification criteria.

  • ML Scorch / ODR: Mooney viscosity (ML 1+4), scorch time (t5), cure curve (ts2, t90, ML/MH torque)
  • Tensile Testing (ISO 37): M100, M300, UTS, elongation at break across supplier variants
  • Rebound Resilience (Schob pendulum): Energy return correlated to rolling resistance performance
  • Statistical Correlation: MWD high-MW tail fraction vs compound processability and final properties across suppliers
PROJECTS

Technical Work

Projects

CVD

CVD Thin Film Operation

Cleanroom operations · In-line metrology · Film uniformity

Semiconductor
Research Aug 2024 – Jan 2025

CVD film uniformity directly drives device performance and yield. Working within a Class 10/100 cleanroom environment, I monitored and correlated process inputs (gas flow ratios, pressure, temperature, RF power) with film output metrics (thickness uniformity, stress, composition) to characterize a reliable and repeatable process window.

  • Deposition methods: Operated thermal CVD and PECVD systems; hands-on with SiN and oxide deposition recipes, with exposure to PVD and ALD process flows
  • Cleanroom protocols: Followed Class 10/100 gowning procedures, particle contamination control, and completed tool qualification checklists prior to each run
  • Metrology: Used ellipsometer for film thickness and optical constants; ran profilometer scans for surface roughness; measured wafer bow for residual stress characterization
  • Process monitoring: Tracked chamber pressure, substrate temperature, precursor gas flow ratios, RF power (PECVD), and deposition time across wafer lots
  • SPC & data analysis: Set up and maintained SPC charts for run-to-run thickness control; built regression models correlating uniformity with gas flow inputs to tighten the process window

SEM
KIT

Semiconductor Process Intelligence Toolkit

Python · PyTorch · Streamlit · SHAP · CNN · TMM · DOE

Semiconductor & AI/ML
Personal Project May 2026

A production-grade, four-module Python & Streamlit toolkit applying real semiconductor engineering workflows to industry-standard datasets: fault detection, optical metrology, wafer defect classification, and process DOE. Deployed live on Streamlit Cloud. Built to advance intelligent manufacturing, yield optimization, and process control across semiconductor fabs.

98%
Fault Detection Accuracy
81%
Wafer Defect Classification
4 nm
Ellipsometry Fit Error
0.702
DOE Desirability Score

Module 1: Wafer Fault Detection (SECOM)

  • Real fab data carries a 14:1 pass/fail imbalance across 1,567 wafers and 591 sensors, making standard classifiers blind to failures. I cleaned features down to 442 meaningful sensors and applied SMOTE balancing to correct the skew before training a Random Forest classifier.
  • SHAP explainability surfaces the exact yield-limiting sensors, not just a black-box score, mirroring the root cause analysis workflow used at TSMC, Micron, and KLA.
  • Result: 98% classification accuracy versus a 93% naive baseline, with interpretable sensor attribution maps ready for process engineer review.

Module 2: Optical Thin Film Metrology

  • Film thickness errors at the nanometer scale shift device performance irreversibly. I built a Transfer Matrix Method (TMM) physics engine to simulate multi-layer optical behavior across 4 stacks (SiO₂, Si₃N₄, HfO₂, a-Si) using real material optical constants.
  • Spectroscopic ellipsometry fitting of Ψ/Δ data achieved a 4 nm fit error with full uniformity mapping across a 49-point wafer grid, matching real metrology throughput constraints.
  • Result: a working metrology simulation environment mirroring CVD/ALD/PVD film characterization workflows at TSMC, Applied Materials, and Lam Research.

Module 3: Wafer Defect Pattern Classifier

  • Manually reviewing 811,000 wafer maps from the WM-811K dataset is infeasible in production, so I trained a custom CNN on 2,249 labeled defect maps spanning 9 pattern classes: edge, scratch, center, donut, local, random, near-full, and none.
  • Grad-CAM spatial localization was layered on top to make defect attribution explainable at the pixel level, reaching 81% classification accuracy and enabling process engineers to trust the model output.
  • Result: a working Automated Defect Classification (ADC) pipeline replicating systems at KLA, Synopsys, and Samsung Semiconductor R&D, targeting a top hiring priority in fab companies for 2025.

Module 4: PECVD Process DOE Optimization

  • No single PECVD parameter can be tuned in isolation: adjusting RF power shifts stress while impacting uniformity. I designed a 2⁴ full factorial experiment (16 runs + 4 center points) across RF power, pressure, SiH₄ flow, and temperature to systematically map four competing responses: deposition rate, uniformity, stress, and refractive index.
  • A multi-response desirability function resolved these tradeoffs, achieving 0.702 desirability at optimal settings with full Pareto tradeoff mapping and process window visualization.
  • Result: hands-on SPC + DOE + process window analysis, the exact workflow called out as required in TSMC, Micron, and Applied Materials process engineer job descriptions.

AI

Sustainable Semiconductor Manufacturing

Machine learning · Digital twins · CHIPS R&D · Intel case study

Semiconductor
Research Jan 2025 – May 2025

I conducted extensive research into sustainable semiconductor manufacturing, focusing on how AI, digital twins, and advanced process control can reduce resource consumption at scale. Semiconductor fabs are among the most resource-intensive industrial facilities: a modern fab consumes 30–50 million gallons of ultrapure water daily and produces thousands of tons of chemical waste annually. Cleanroom HVAC systems alone account for 40–50% of total energy consumption. As the industry scales with AI/HPC demand, sustainability pressures and regulatory requirements (CHIPS Act R&D mandates) make resource optimization a technical and strategic priority — and this research explored how data-driven tools can meaningfully close that gap.

Key Technical Areas Covered

  • Digital Twins: Virtual fab replicas enabling process simulation, predictive maintenance scheduling, and energy optimization without production disruption
  • ML for Process Control: Predictive models for yield forecasting, anomaly detection in metrology data, and recipe optimization
  • Water Recycling: Ultrapure water consumption reduction through predictive demand management and closed-loop recycling systems
  • Chemical Waste Reduction: AI-driven slurry and etchant concentration monitoring for near-zero-waste CMP and wet etch processes
  • CHIPS R&D Program: Federal framework implications for fab sustainability R&D investment and regulatory reporting requirements
Machine LearningDigital TwinsPredictive AnalyticsFab SustainabilityCHIPS ActIntel Case StudyEnergy Management
ICD

Li-ion Battery Design for ICD

LiC₆ | LiMnO₂ | LiPF₆ · SEI modeling · Thermal management · Medical-grade

Battery & Energy
Research Aug – Dec 2024

Designed a complete Li-ion battery system for an implantable cardioverter defibrillator (ICD), balancing four conflicting constraints: ≥2-year operational life, 5–7A pulse discharge for defibrillation, full biocompatibility for permanent implantation, and stable performance at 37°C body temperature — where SEI aging runs 15–20% faster than at room temperature.

Chemistry & Design Rationale

  • LiC₆ anode: Stable SEI formation, low Li plating risk, excellent cycle life
  • LiMnO₂ cathode: Superior thermal stability vs LiCoO₂, biocompatible and safe for implant
  • LiPF₆/EC/DMC electrolyte: Stable SEI (EC), low viscosity at 37°C (DMC), high ionic conductivity
  • Degradation modeled via Arrhenius kinetics and parabolic SEI growth, validated ≥75% charge retention over 730 days

SEI Growth Physics

The Solid Electrolyte Interphase (SEI) forms on the graphite anode surface as electrolyte reduction products deposit during the first few charge cycles. This layer grows continuously throughout battery life as fresh electrolyte diffuses through existing SEI to the anode surface. At 37°C body temperature, the Arrhenius-enhanced growth rate is approximately 15–20% higher than at 25°C, requiring careful calendar life modeling.

Thermal Analysis — Pulse Discharge

  • Heat generation during 7A pulse: Q = I²R × t_pulse (Joule heating dominant)
  • Titanium casing: k = 22 W/mK, provides structural integrity with moderate thermal conduction to surrounding tissue
  • Body tissue thermal coupling: convective boundary condition, ~37°C ambient
  • Peak temperature rise during pulse: calculated to remain within 2–3°C of body temperature; no tissue damage risk
  • Developed transient conjugate heat-transfer model in COMSOL for prismatic Li-ion battery pack thermal management. Optimized cooling strategy to minimize hotspots and temperature non-uniformity.

COMSOL simulation — fluid channel cross-section for prismatic Li-ion pack thermal management

FIX

Thermal Resistance Test Fixture Design

SolidWorks Flow Simulation · FEA · Serpentine cold plate · CNC manufacturing

Mechanical Design
Academic Purdue 2025

RθJC (junction-to-case thermal resistance) is one of the key thermal metrics used to qualify semiconductor packages — it defines how efficiently heat moves from the die to the package case under controlled cooling. Getting an accurate, repeatable measurement is harder than it looks: traditional thermocouple-based methods are sensitive to contact pressure, surface roughness, and where exactly the probe sits on the case, all of which introduce variability between test runs. This project focused on designing a fixture that eliminates those variables through mechanical consistency — uniform clamping force, a stable isothermal cold plate, and a controlled TIM layer — so the measured RθJC reflects the package, not the test setup.

Design Components

  • Copper Cold Plate (C101): Serpentine channels at 0.9 w/h ratio provide efficient, uniform heat extraction; keeps the cold plate surface isothermal so the thermal gradient across the package footprint stays consistent run to run
  • Aluminum Load Frame (6061-T6): Calibrated springs apply controlled, repeatable contact pressure (±5 mm height adjustment); eliminates the contact resistance variability that makes traditional fixture results hard to reproduce
  • Thermal Interface Material: Thermal grease (k = 5 W/mK) fills surface asperities at the package-to-cold plate interface — ensures the dominant thermal resistance in the measurement path is the package itself, not the contact

Validation Workflow

  • SolidWorks 3D model of full assembly including spring preload mechanism
  • Flow Simulation: k-ε turbulence model, 2 L/min inlet, 25°C water, 100W heat load
  • FEA static analysis: spring force distribution, deflection under compression load
  • GD&T technical drawings generated for CNC shop

RθJC test fixture — cross-section assembly (SolidWorks / Flow Simulation): copper cold plate serpentine channels, Al 6061-T6 load frame, calibrated spring preload, and TIM layer

FEA

Off-Road Buggy

Tubular steel frame · Impact analysis · Load path optimization · University of Twente

Mechanical Design
Academic 2020 – 2023

Designed a complete off-road buggy frame from scratch, optimizing for structural integrity under real-world impact loading scenarios (frontal impact, rear impact, rollover) while minimizing weight and meeting fabrication constraints for welded tubular steel construction.

  • Frame topology: Triangulated space frame; axial-only member loads, maximizing strength-to-weight
  • FEA load cases: 2g frontal, 1.5g rear, rollover (1g+0.5g lateral), combined corner+braking
  • Critical joint redesign: Main hoop/lower rail weld: 85% → 52% yield strength with gusset plate
  • SolidWorks workflow: 3D frame → FEA mesh → BCs (wheel contact fixed) → Von Mises/deflection output

FEA load analysis — triangulated frame under multi-axis impact loading with critical joint highlighted

Physical buggy frame build — tubular steel space frame with suspension, drivetrain, and roll cage integrated

CFD

Train Carriage Pathogen Flow Optimization

OpenFOAM · MATLAB · CFD · Ventilation redesign · University of Twente

Mechanical Design
Academic Sep – Nov 2021

Modeled pathogen spread and heat loss in train carriages using CFD, applying fluid mechanics to simulate airflow patterns around passenger seating configurations. Redesigned ventilation geometry to minimize high-risk airflow recirculation zones and reduce passenger exposure to airborne pathogens.

  • Modeled pathogen transport in a train carriage cross-section using OpenFOAM CFD simulation
  • Analyzed streamline patterns to identify high-risk airflow recirculation and transmission zones
  • Proposed seating layout and ventilation modifications to reduce pathogen concentration
